title: Managed Kubernetes Node Auto-Repair Frequency, Premature Replacement Signal, and Repair-Storm Risk
exclusions: Does not replace UC-3.2.3 kubelet NotReady dwell and lease analytics, UC-3.2.33 voluntary drain and eviction audit narratives, or UC-3.2.46 Cluster Autoscaler and Karpenter scale-out failure against Pending backlog. Does not, by itself, prove VPC routing, data-plane packet loss, or storage faults without sibling network and datastore telemetry. Windows node pools with different audit field shapes may require vendor-specific macros. Does not automatically remediate pools or freeze rollouts; it produces prioritized evidence for human runbooks.
evidence: Saved search uc_3_2_44_managed_node_auto_repair_storm with hourly schedule; versioned node_instance_launched.csv when premature replacement math is required; weekly CSV export of the closing table to a restricted evidence index with linked CloudTrail, GCP audit, or Azure Activity query IDs.

controlTest.positiveScenario: In a non-production managed pool, induce or simulate a provider-observed unhealthy instance so CloudTrail records RecycleNode or termination with InstanceUnhealthy context, or trigger GKE node auto-repair delete telemetry, or reproduce an AKS RepairNode operation in a lab subscription; ensure Splunk ingests the event with cluster and pool dimensions, run uc_3_2_44_managed_node_auto_repair_storm, and confirm repair_detail populates, repairs_rolling_1h increments across sequential replacements, and repair_storm_flag becomes one when five events land inside one hour for the same pool.
controlTest.negativeScenario: During a cordon-only maintenance drill without cloud recycle APIs firing, or during pure Cluster Autoscaler scale-out failures without managed repair events, confirm this saved search returns no managed_cloud_repair rows while UC-3.2.33 or UC-3.2.46 continue to carry the correct primary signal, proving sibling separation.

dataSources: index=aws index=gcp index=azure index=cloud or consolidated security-audit index with sourcetype=aws:cloudtrail for Amazon EKS managed node group RecycleNode, autoscaling EC2 termination, and InstanceUnhealthy style narratives; sourcetype=google:gcp:audit for GKE node auto-repair correlated compute.instance.delete or compute.googleapis.com/instance.deleted records carrying NODE_AUTO_REPAIR semantics; sourcetype=azure:auditlogs for AKS CloudProvisioningServices.RepairNode and AKS_MaintenanceUnscheduled style operations; index=k8s_logs or index=k8s with sourcetype=kube:container:logs, sourcetype=karpenter, or otel-normalized controller logs for Karpenter Disruption.NodeClaim decisions (Drift, Underutilized, Empty, Expired, Health); index=k8s_metrics sourcetype=prometheus:scrape:metrics for kube_node_info pool labels and node cardinality, kube_node_status_condition Ready transitions for follow-up NotReady within ten minutes on replacement candidates, and kube_pod_info for workload-on-node root-cause hints; Splunk Connect for Syslog or HEC paths that land cloud audit and Kubernetes logs with consistent cluster dimensions; optional governance lookup node_instance_launched.csv keyed on cluster, instance_hint, and node_name with launch_epoch seconds for premature replacement math.
app: Splunk Add-on for Amazon Web Services (CloudTrail modular input), Splunk Add-on for Google Cloud Platform (Pub/Sub or GCS audit export), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services or Azure Monitor export to HEC, Splunk Connect for Syslog for multi-vendor syslog and JSON CEF forwarding, Splunk OpenTelemetry Collector for Kubernetes shipping Karpenter controller logs and kube-state-metrics scrapes; Splunk Enterprise 9.2+ or Splunk Cloud for streamstats time_window correlation.
spl:
`comment("UC-3.2.44 Managed Kubernetes auto-repair frequency, premature replacement, repair-storm. Tunables: earliest=-24h@h latest=now; storm_threshold=5 per 3600s; premature_sec=3600; adjust index macros for aws gcp azure k8s")`
| multisearch
    [ search (index=aws OR index=cloud OR index=main) sourcetype="aws:cloudtrail" earliest=-24h@h latest=now
      | eval ev=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(eventName,event_name,""))))
      | eval rawl=lower(_raw)
      | eval cluster=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(eks_cluster_name,cluster,requestParameters_clusterName,recipientAccountId,"unknown_cluster"))))
      | where ev="recyclenode" OR match(rawl,"recyclenode") OR (match(rawl,"terminat") AND match(rawl,"instanceunhealthy")) OR (match(rawl,"managednodegroup") AND match(rawl,"unhealth"))
      | eval cloud_plane="aws"
      | eval repair_class="managed_cloud_repair"
      | eval repair_detail=case(ev=="recyclenode","eks_mng_recycle_node", match(rawl,"instanceunhealthy"),"ec2_instance_unhealthy_termination", true(),"aws_mng_repair_adjacent")
      | eval node_pool=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(requestParameters_nodegroupName,nodegroupName,requestParameters_resources,"unknown_pool"))))
      | eval instance_hint=trim(toString(coalesce(instanceId,requestParameters_instanceId,responseElements_instancesSet_items_0_instanceId,"")))
      | eval node_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(kubernetes_node_name,node_name,""))))
      | fields _time cluster node_pool cloud_plane repair_class repair_detail instance_hint node_name ]
    [ search (index=gcp OR index=cloud OR index=main) sourcetype="google:gcp:audit" earliest=-24h@h latest=now
      | eval rawl=lower(_raw)
      | eval cluster=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(resource_labels_cluster_name,labels_cluster_name,protoPayload_resourceName,"unknown_cluster"))))
      | where (match(rawl,"compute.instances.delete") OR match(rawl,"instance.deleted")) AND match(rawl,"node_auto_repair")
      | eval cloud_plane="gcp"
      | eval repair_class="managed_cloud_repair"
      | eval repair_detail="gke_node_auto_repair_delete"
      | eval node_pool=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(resource_labels_node_pool,resource_labels_nodepool,protoPayload_metadata_node_pool,"unknown_pool"))))
      | rex field=_raw max_match=0 "(?i)\"instance_id\"\\s*:\\s*\"(?<gcp_inst>[^\"]+)\""
      | eval instance_hint=trim(toString(coalesce(gcp_inst,resource_labels_instance_id,"")))
      | eval node_name=""
      | fields _time cluster node_pool cloud_plane repair_class repair_detail instance_hint node_name ]
    [ search (index=azure OR index=cloud OR index=main) sourcetype="azure:auditlogs" earliest=-24h@h latest=now
      | eval rawl=lower(_raw)
      | eval op=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(OperationNameValue,operationName,ActivityDisplayName,""))))
      | eval cluster=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(properties_resourceName,properties_clusterName,_ResourceId,"unknown_cluster"))))
      | where match(rawl,"repairnode") OR match(rawl,"cloudprovisioningservices.repairnode") OR match(rawl,"aks_maintenanceunscheduled")
      | eval cloud_plane="azure"
      | eval repair_class="managed_cloud_repair"
      | eval repair_detail=case(match(rawl,"repairnode"),"aks_repair_node", true(),"aks_unscheduled_maintenance_signal")
      | eval node_pool=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(properties_agentPool,properties_nodepool,properties_nodePool,"unknown_pool"))))
      | eval instance_hint=trim(toString(coalesce(properties_vmId,properties_instanceId,"")))
      | eval node_name=""
      | fields _time cluster node_pool cloud_plane repair_class repair_detail instance_hint node_name ]
    [ search (index=k8s_logs OR index=k8s OR index=containers) (sourcetype="kube:container:logs" OR sourcetype="karpenter" OR sourcetype="otel:logs") earliest=-24h@h latest=now
      | eval rawl=lower(_raw)
      | eval cluster=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(k8s_cluster_name,cluster_name,cluster,"unknown_cluster"))))
      | where (like(rawl,"%karpenter%") OR sourcetype="karpenter") AND match(rawl,"disruption") AND (match(rawl,"nodeclaim") OR match(rawl,"node_claim") OR match(rawl,"drift") OR match(rawl,"underutilized") OR match(rawl,"empty") OR match(rawl,"expired") OR match(rawl,"health"))
      | eval cloud_plane="karpenter"
      | eval repair_class=case(match(rawl,"drift") OR match(rawl,"underutilized") OR match(rawl,"empty") OR match(rawl,"expired"),"karpenter_optimization_disruption", true(),"karpenter_health_or_ambiguous")
      | eval repair_detail=case(match(rawl,"drift"),"karpenter_drift", match(rawl,"underutilized"),"karpenter_underutilized", match(rawl,"empty"),"karpenter_empty", match(rawl,"expired"),"karpenter_expired", match(rawl,"health"),"karpenter_health", true(),"karpenter_disruption_other")
      | eval node_pool=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(karpenter_nodepool,node_pool,provisioner_name,"karpenter_aggregate"))))
      | rex field=_raw max_match=0 "(?i)node[:=\\s]+(?<kn>[a-z0-9\\.\\-]+)"
      | eval node_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(kn,k8s_node_name,""))))
      | eval instance_hint=""
      | fields _time cluster node_pool cloud_plane repair_class repair_detail instance_hint node_name ]
| fillnull value="unknown_cluster" cluster
| fillnull value="unknown_pool" node_pool
| fillnull value="" node_name instance_hint
| sort 0 cluster, node_pool, + _time
| streamstats time_window=3600s count AS repairs_rolling_1h BY cluster node_pool
| eval repair_storm_flag=if(repairs_rolling_1h>=5,1,0)
| eventstats count AS repairs_per_pool_day BY cluster node_pool
| join type=left max=0 cluster node_pool
    [| search index=k8s_metrics sourcetype="prometheus:scrape:metrics" earliest=-24h@h latest=now
      | eval cluster=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(k8s_cluster_name,cluster_name,cluster,eks_cluster_name,gke_cluster_name,aks_cluster_name,resource_cluster,""))))
      | eval mn=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(__name__,metric_name,name,""))))
      | eval nn=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(node,node_name,kubernetes_io_hostname,k8s_node_name,""))))
      | rex field=_raw "node_group=\"(?<nglab>[^\"]+)\""
      | eval node_pool=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(nglab,label_eks_nodegroup,label_node_group,node_group,nodegroup,""))))
      | where mn="kube_node_info" AND len(cluster)>0 AND len(nn)>0 AND len(node_pool)>0
      | stats dc(nn) AS kube_pool_live_nodes BY cluster node_pool ]
| join type=left max=0 cluster node_name
    [| search index=k8s_metrics sourcetype="prometheus:scrape:metrics" earliest=-24h@h latest=now
      | eval cluster=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(k8s_cluster_name,cluster_name,cluster,eks_cluster_name,gke_cluster_name,aks_cluster_name,resource_cluster,""))))
      | eval mn=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(__name__,metric_name,name,""))))
      | eval nn=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(node,node_name,kubernetes_io_hostname,k8s_node_name,""))))
      | where mn="kube_node_status_condition" AND len(cluster)>0 AND len(nn)>0
      | rex field=_raw "condition=\"(?<cond>[^\"]+)\""
      | rex field=_raw "status=\"(?<st>[^\"]+)\""
      | where cond="Ready" AND lower(st)="false"
      | stats latest(_time) AS last_ready_false BY cluster nn
      | rename nn AS node_name ]
| join type=left max=0 cluster instance_hint node_name
    [| inputlookup node_instance_launched.csv
      | eval cluster=lower(trim(toString(cluster)))
      | eval instance_hint=trim(toString(coalesce(instance_hint,instance_id,vm_id,"")))
      | eval node_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(node_name,node,""))))
      | eval launch_epoch=tonumber(tostring(coalesce(launch_epoch,created_epoch,"0")),10)
      | fields cluster instance_hint node_name launch_epoch ]
| eval premature_replace_flag=if(isnotnull(launch_epoch) AND launch_epoch>0 AND (_time-launch_epoch)<3600 AND _time>launch_epoch,1,0)
| eval followup_notready_risk_sec=if(isnotnull(last_ready_false), round(_time-last_ready_false,0), null())
| eval workload_rca_note=if(len(node_name)>0,printf("map kube_pod_info node=%s for workload owners",node_name),"attach kube_pod_info join for workload RCA")
| eval dm_key="uc3244"
| join type=left max=0 dm_key
    [| tstats count AS change_window_hits FROM datamodel=Change WHERE nodename=Change earliest=-24h@h latest=now
      | eval dm_key="uc3244" ]
| fields - dm_key
| fillnull value=0 change_window_hits kube_pool_live_nodes repair_storm_flag premature_replace_flag
| eval severity=case(repair_storm_flag==1 AND repair_class=="managed_cloud_repair","high_repair_storm_cloud", repair_storm_flag==1 AND cloud_plane=="karpenter","medium_karpenter_burst_review", premature_replace_flag==1 AND repair_class=="managed_cloud_repair","high_premature_cloud_replace", match(repair_detail,"karpenter_health"),"medium_karpenter_health_review", repairs_per_pool_day>=12 AND repair_class=="managed_cloud_repair","medium_elevated_daily_repair_rate", true(),"low_informational_repair_event")
| table _time cluster node_pool cloud_plane repair_class repair_detail repairs_rolling_1h repair_storm_flag repairs_per_pool_day kube_pool_live_nodes premature_replace_flag followup_notready_risk_sec workload_rca_note change_window_hits severity
description: Unique monitoring axis: managed node-pool auto-repair and replacement telemetry from cloud audit planes plus Karpenter disruption decisions, not one-shot kubelet NotReady dwell (UC-3.2.3), not voluntary cordon and drain governance (UC-3.2.33), and not Cluster Autoscaler or Karpenter scale-out refusal against Pending backlog (UC-3.2.46). The analytic multisearches AWS CloudTrail, GCP Cloud Audit, Azure Activity, and Karpenter-shaped controller logs into a common repair-event schema, rolls a one-hour replacement velocity per cluster and node pool with streamstats, flags repair storms when five or more replacements appear inside the same sliding hour, enriches kube_node_info live pool membership for baseline context, optionally joins node_instance_launched.csv to mark premature replacements under one hour of observed life, fuses Karpenter Drift and Underutilized lanes as optimization-class disruption distinct from Health-like repair mimicry, and leaves hooks for kube_node_status_condition Ready=false samples on correlated nodes inside a ten-minute follow-up window so responders see whether replacements immediately re-enter instability.
value: Platform leaders gain an evidence-backed narrative that separates healthy automation from dangerous oscillation: a rising repair rate in one pool after an AMI change, a subnet glitch, or a CNI regression shows up as velocity and storm flags before customer-facing error budgets burn. FinOps and reliability partners can justify pausing rollouts or freezing image pipelines when premature replacement counts spike, because the same telemetry names the cloud plane, pool, and signal class without manual console archaeology. Audit and incident reviewers retain defensible timelines tying provider repair actions to workload hints and optional launch-age lookups, which accelerates root cause analysis when multiple teams argue about symptoms versus causes during regional degradations.
implementation: Land aws:cloudtrail, google:gcp:audit, azure:auditlogs, and Karpenter controller logs on consistent indexes with cluster and pool dimensions; scrape kube-state-metrics kube_node_info into k8s_metrics; publish optional node_instance_launched.csv for age math. Save uc_3_2_44_managed_node_auto_repair_storm hourly with earliest=-24h@h latest=now. Route repair_storm_flag and premature_replace_flag rows to platform on-call with cloud console drill links.
visualization: Timechart of repairs_rolling_1h by cluster and node_pool; single value of repair_storm_flag incidents per day; table mirroring the closing SPL projection with drilldowns to raw aws:cloudtrail, google:gcp:audit, azure:auditlogs, and karpenter logs; overlay kube_pool_live_nodes for capacity context.
cimSpl:
| tstats summariesonly=true latest(Change.action) AS change_action latest(Change.object) AS change_object count AS change_events FROM datamodel=Change WHERE nodename=Change earliest=-24h@h latest=now BY Change.user
| join type=left max=0 change_object
    [| tstats summariesonly=true latest(Application_State.state) AS app_state latest(Application_State.info) AS app_info FROM datamodel=Application_State WHERE nodename=Application_State (Application_State.app="kubernetes" OR Application_State.app="k8s" OR like(Application_State.app,"%kube%")) earliest=-24h@h latest=now BY Application_State.dest
     | rename Application_State.dest AS change_object ]
| where change_events>0 AND (like(lower(app_state),"%notready%") OR like(lower(app_info),"%repair%") OR like(lower(app_info),"%replace%"))
| table Change.user change_object change_events change_action app_state app_info
dataModelAcceleration: Accelerate Splunk CIM Change summaries for cloud automation principals and Application_State summaries mapped to Kubernetes nodes so tstats overlays stay warm when correlating provider repair operations with node readiness swings; retain at least thirty days for quarterly reliability reviews.

references.title: Google Cloud — Repair unhealthy nodes in GKE (node auto-repair)
Amazon EKS — Managed node groups (update and recycle behavior)
Microsoft Learn — Automatically repair Azure Kubernetes Service nodes
Karpenter — Disruption concepts (Drift, Empty, Expired, health)
Amazon EKS — Control plane logging (audit and API diagnostics)
AWS CloudTrail — Logging EKS API activity
Splunk Connect for Syslog — Container and Kubernetes logging patterns

knownFalsePositives: Legitimate auto-repair after genuine hardware faults or host hypervisor defects produces the same cloud audit signatures as software regressions; require velocity, storm clustering, or premature-age context before treating a single replacement as incident-grade. Planned maintenance windows and provider-published infrastructure events often include preemptive node recycling; cross-check maintenance calendars and Azure Activity informational categories before paging application teams. Intentional rolling node-pool upgrades that surge new instances then retire old ones are change-driven replacements, not reactive repair; align timestamps with recorded upgrades and UC-3.2.33 drain tickets so upgrades do not masquerade as storms. Karpenter Drift following an AMI or image bump is optimization-class disruption and should be labeled FinOps or image hygiene rather than cloud repair unless Health reasons dominate the same interval. Spot or preemptible reclaim termination events resemble repair-storm bursts but are cloud capacity reclaim, not Kubernetes health repair; tag instance_market_option or preemptible labels when forwarding CloudTrail. Tier-aware autoscaling that cordons and replaces nodes deliberately can emit dense recycle sequences; map those controllers in lookups before declaring subnet faults. Transient ENI or CNI attach failures sometimes trigger one-off managed repairs that self-heal; dampen severity when follow-up NotReady risk stays empty for thirty minutes. Recently rolled-back AMI bugs can cause short repair spikes that stop after revert; correlate with image change records. Silent OS kernel auto-updates that reboot workers may surface as termination and recreate pairs unrelated to application defects; pair with patch baselines. Regional cloud degradation that concentrates replacements in one availability zone can look like a pool storm even when the root cause is upstream networking; compare AZ distribution in raw events. Deliberate kubectl drain followed by managed auto-repair overlaps voluntary and involuntary planes; require UC-3.2.33 absence of matching tickets before blaming automation alone. Image pull failures that drive kubelet NotReady cycles can precede managed recycle loops; escalate only when cloud repair signals lead the loop rather than lagging image errors.

premiumApps.name: Splunk ITSI
premiumApps.note: optional — bind repair-storm episodes to node-pool entities when replacement velocity breaches local SLO budgets

detailedImplementation:
Step 1 — Prerequisites

Head of Platform owns this control together with cloud infrastructure engineers who certify CloudTrail, GCP audit, and Azure Activity pipelines, Kubernetes platform site reliability engineers who operate kube-state-metrics and Splunk OpenTelemetry Collector, and incident commanders who arbitrate paging when repair velocity overlaps customer incidents. This use case occupies a narrow monitoring axis: managed cloud repair and replacement telemetry for worker pools, Karpenter disruption decisions that may resemble repair, and correlation hooks into kube_node_info baselines—not kubelet silence without provider recycle evidence (UC-3.2.3), not voluntary cordon and Eviction API governance (UC-3.2.33), and not autoscaler refusal to add capacity while pods stay Pending (UC-3.2.46). Evidence must remain trustworthy when three clouds and Karpenter coexist in one estate, so field normalization and lookup governance matter as much as SPL cleverness.

Index and token contracts: dedicate partitions for aws:cloudtrail, google:gcp:audit, and azure:auditlogs with least-privilege roles that let platform teams read resource identifiers while redacting sensitive request payloads in executive dashboards. Keep Karpenter and cluster-autoscaler adjacent logs in k8s_logs or k8s with sourcetype discipline so Disruption.NodeClaim narratives do not drown inside generic application streams. Keep kube-state-metrics scrapes in k8s_metrics with sourcetype prometheus:scrape:metrics and consistent cluster labels that coalesce eks_cluster_name, gke_cluster_name, and aks_cluster_name aliases into one cluster dimension for joins. Issue distinct HEC tokens per index class, rotate quarterly, and document residency rules when EU clusters must not land in US search peers.

Cloud audit prerequisites: AWS organizations should enable organization trails or account trails that include EKS-related management events relevant to managed node group lifecycle, including RecycleNode and autoscaling termination stories; validate that eventName and requestParameters fields are not truncated by intermediary filters. GCP projects exporting data access and admin activity logs must include compute.instance.delete or compute.instances.delete narratives with reason metadata that mentions NODE_AUTO_REPAIR when auto-repair fires; Pub/Sub subscriptions need dead-letter handling so Splunk never silently loses bursts during regional incidents. Azure subscriptions must emit Resource Manager operations for AKS repair workflows including CloudProvisioningServices.RepairNode and maintenance signals such as AKS_MaintenanceUnscheduled when your tenant logs them; confirm Activity Log diagnostic settings reach the same Splunk index modeled in this UC.

Kubernetes metrics prerequisites: kube-state-metrics must expose kube_node_info with node pool or node group labels that match cloud console names after lowercase normalization; without that alignment, pool-level storm detection devolves to cluster-wide noise. kube_node_status_condition for Ready=false supports follow-up correlation within roughly ten minutes of a replacement event; scrape intervals of fifteen to thirty seconds balance fidelity against license cost. If you omit kube_node_created_seconds or an equivalent birth-time signal, premature replacement detection relies entirely on node_instance_launched.csv populated from your cloud inventory jobs.

Lookup governance: maintain node_instance_launched.csv under change control with columns cluster, node_name, instance_hint, launch_epoch, owner_team, and optional az. Refresh the file when autoscaling groups or MIGs rehydrate instances after scale-to-zero events. Evidence packs should cite commit hashes for the CSV exactly as other gold Kubernetes controls cite maintenance lookups.

Risk briefing: repair storms often indicate an AMI regression, a CNI or ENI defect concentrated in one zone, or a quota and throttling loop—not merely bad application code. Premature replacement under one hour of life is a strong hint that the new instance class or image is unhealthy or that bootstrap joins fail quickly. Karpenter Drift and Underutilized disruption are frequently desired cost optimization; only Health-heavy bursts should be treated like cloud repair without FinOps context.

Licensing and volume: CloudTrail and multi-cloud audit ingestion can dominate daily volume during large fleet churn; use SC4S or collector-side filtering to drop irrelevant S3 object events while preserving compute lifecycle narratives. Prometheus scrapes multiply with node counts; cap label cardinality before enabling experimental well-known labels on custom metrics.

Training: teach responders the semantic difference between EKS RecycleNode and EC2 generic termination, between GKE auto-repair deletes and human gcloud deletes, and between AKS repair operations and planned control plane upgrades. Teach FinOps partners that Karpenter Drift after image bumps is expected while Health disruption may still merit paging.

Review cadence: quarterly replay one historical repair storm after cloud provider SDK upgrades because field flattening occasionally renames requestParameters paths. Escalation alignment: high_repair_storm_cloud pages platform and cloud bridges together; medium_karpenter_burst_review routes to platform first with FinOps cc when optimization signals dominate.

Differentiation recap: managed auto-repair frequency, premature replacement, and storm risk are in scope; NotReady-only detection, voluntary drain auditing, and scale-out refusal are explicitly out of scope as primary detectors.

Step 2 — Configure data collection

Land AWS CloudTrail through the Splunk AWS add-on modular input or through Splunk Connect for Syslog when your security operations standardizes on syslog TLS fan-in. Normalize sourcetype to aws:cloudtrail and preserve eventName, requestParameters, recipientAccountId, and userIdentity. For EKS-heavy accounts, add saved-field extractions for eksClusterName and nodegroupName when those fields appear as JSON paths rather than flat keys.

Land GCP Cloud Audit logs through the Splunk GCP add-on Pub/Sub input or through a security sink to Cloud Storage followed by batch ingest. Ensure protoPayload.methodName, resource labels for cluster and node pool, and textual reason fragments for NODE_AUTO_REPAIR survive parsing. Map cluster names to the same lowercase convention used in kube-state-metrics external labels.

Land Azure Activity and resource logs through Event Hub and the Azure add-on or through your enterprise Azure Monitor export. Flatten OperationNameValue, properties.agentPool, and properties.clusterName consistently. When multiple subscriptions feed one index, include subscription_id in cluster composite keys in a local macro if collisions occur.

Configure Splunk OpenTelemetry Collector filelog receivers for karpenter namespace pods or kube-system installations that embed Karpenter logs, with regex includes for disruption decisions. Export through splunk_hec into k8s_logs with sourcetype kube:container:logs or karpenter when you choose a dedicated sourcetype. Pair logs with k8s_events receiver only when Node events add context; the primary signal for this UC remains cloud audit plus Karpenter controller narratives.

Prometheus scrape job or OTEL prometheus receiver should target kube-state-metrics with honor_labels true and relabel rules that propagate eks.amazonaws.com/nodegroup, cloud.google.com/gke-nodepool, or agentpool labels into node_group label dimensions consumable by the kube_node_info arm in Step 3.

Example OTEL collector fragment emphasizing audit and metrics parity:

```yaml
receivers:
  filelog/karpenter:
    include:
      - /var/log/pods/karpenter_*/*/*.log
    operators:
      - type: json_parser
        timestamp:
          parse_from: attributes.time
  prometheus_simple/ksm:
    collection_interval: 30s
    endpoint: kube-state-metrics.kube-system.svc.cluster.local:8080/metrics
exporters:
  splunk_hec/logs:
    token: ${SPLUNK_HEC_TOKEN_K8S_LOGS}
    endpoint: https://splunk-hec.example:8088/services/collector/event
    index: k8s_logs
    sourcetype: kube:container:logs
  splunk_hec/metrics:
    token: ${SPLUNK_HEC_TOKEN_K8S_METRICS}
    endpoint: https://splunk-hec.example:8088/services/collector/event
    index: k8s_metrics
    sourcetype: prometheus:scrape:metrics
service:
  pipelines:
    logs/karpenter:
      receivers: [filelog/karpenter]
      exporters: [splunk_hec/logs]
    metrics/ksm:
      receivers: [prometheus_simple/ksm]
      exporters: [splunk_hec/metrics]
```

Publish node_instance_launched.csv sample rows aligned to your cloud inventory job:

```csv
cluster,node_name,instance_hint,launch_epoch,owner_team
prod-eks-01,ip-10-0-3-21.ec2.internal,i-0abc123def4567890,1745688000,platform-compute
prod-gke-01,gke-prod-pool-n1-abc12-node-7d3f,projects/p/zones/us-central1-a/instances/gke-node-1,1745688300,platform-gke
```

Validation searches before alert authoring: index search for sourcetype=aws:cloudtrail eventName=RecycleNode earliest=-24h; sourcetype=google:gcp:audit with node_auto_repair earliest=-24h; sourcetype=azure:auditlogs with repairnode earliest=-24h; karpenter logs with disruption earliest=-24h; k8s_metrics kube_node_info earliest=-24h. Skew between cloud _time and metrics _time should remain under two minutes for meaningful followup_notready_risk_sec math.

Security: restrict who can read raw CloudTrail S3 ARNs in dashboards. Redact customer-identifying pod names in executive exports while keeping hashed identifiers for correlation.

Step 3 — Create the search and alert

Save as uc_3_2_44_managed_node_auto_repair_storm with hourly schedule, dispatch earliest=-24h@h, dispatch latest=now, and throttle duplicate cluster and node_pool pages for forty-five minutes unless severity escalates from medium to high. Include repairs_rolling_1h, repair_storm_flag, and premature_replace_flag in pager bodies with direct links to cloud console node pool health views.

Pipeline narrative for reviewers: the opening comment macro lists tunables for indexes and thresholds. multisearch fans four parallel arms so missing GCP data does not silence AWS evidence: aws:cloudtrail captures RecycleNode and InstanceUnhealthy-flavored terminations; google:gcp:audit captures GKE node auto-repair deletes; azure:auditlogs captures AKS repair and unscheduled maintenance signals; kube container or karpenter sourcetypes capture Karpenter disruption classes. coalesce and case ladders normalize cluster, node_pool, repair_detail, and repair_class so downstream stats behave consistently. After sorting by cluster, node_pool, and _time, streamstats with time_window=3600s counts replacements inside a sliding hour per pool for storm detection. eventstats adds repairs_per_pool_day as a coarse daily rate proxy inside the window. join enriches kube_node_info cardinality into kube_pool_live_nodes for capacity context. A second join against kube_node_status_condition Ready=false provides followup_notready_risk_sec when node names align. inputlookup node_instance_launched.csv adds launch_epoch for premature_replace_flag when your inventory supplies trustworthy birth times. A tstats arm against the Change data model supplies change_window_hits as a gentle cross-plane hint that automation is active during the same day without claiming every change caused a repair. The closing case statement sets severity for storm, premature cloud replacements, Karpenter health emphasis, and elevated daily rates. workload_rca_note reminds analysts to map kube_pod_info for owners when node_name is present.

Paste-and-run SPL for alerts and dashboards must match the spl JSON field exactly:

```spl
`comment("UC-3.2.44 Managed Kubernetes auto-repair frequency, premature replacement, repair-storm. Tunables: earliest=-24h@h latest=now; storm_threshold=5 per 3600s; premature_sec=3600; adjust index macros for aws gcp azure k8s")`
| multisearch
    [ search (index=aws OR index=cloud OR index=main) sourcetype="aws:cloudtrail" earliest=-24h@h latest=now
      | eval ev=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(eventName,event_name,""))))
      | eval rawl=lower(_raw)
      | eval cluster=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(eks_cluster_name,cluster,requestParameters_clusterName,recipientAccountId,"unknown_cluster"))))
      | where ev="recyclenode" OR match(rawl,"recyclenode") OR (match(rawl,"terminat") AND match(rawl,"instanceunhealthy")) OR (match(rawl,"managednodegroup") AND match(rawl,"unhealth"))
      | eval cloud_plane="aws"
      | eval repair_class="managed_cloud_repair"
      | eval repair_detail=case(ev=="recyclenode","eks_mng_recycle_node", match(rawl,"instanceunhealthy"),"ec2_instance_unhealthy_termination", true(),"aws_mng_repair_adjacent")
      | eval node_pool=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(requestParameters_nodegroupName,nodegroupName,requestParameters_resources,"unknown_pool"))))
      | eval instance_hint=trim(toString(coalesce(instanceId,requestParameters_instanceId,responseElements_instancesSet_items_0_instanceId,"")))
      | eval node_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(kubernetes_node_name,node_name,""))))
      | fields _time cluster node_pool cloud_plane repair_class repair_detail instance_hint node_name ]
    [ search (index=gcp OR index=cloud OR index=main) sourcetype="google:gcp:audit" earliest=-24h@h latest=now
      | eval rawl=lower(_raw)
      | eval cluster=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(resource_labels_cluster_name,labels_cluster_name,protoPayload_resourceName,"unknown_cluster"))))
      | where (match(rawl,"compute.instances.delete") OR match(rawl,"instance.deleted")) AND match(rawl,"node_auto_repair")
      | eval cloud_plane="gcp"
      | eval repair_class="managed_cloud_repair"
      | eval repair_detail="gke_node_auto_repair_delete"
      | eval node_pool=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(resource_labels_node_pool,resource_labels_nodepool,protoPayload_metadata_node_pool,"unknown_pool"))))
      | rex field=_raw max_match=0 "(?i)\"instance_id\"\\s*:\\s*\"(?<gcp_inst>[^\"]+)\""
      | eval instance_hint=trim(toString(coalesce(gcp_inst,resource_labels_instance_id,"")))
      | eval node_name=""
      | fields _time cluster node_pool cloud_plane repair_class repair_detail instance_hint node_name ]
    [ search (index=azure OR index=cloud OR index=main) sourcetype="azure:auditlogs" earliest=-24h@h latest=now
      | eval rawl=lower(_raw)
      | eval op=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(OperationNameValue,operationName,ActivityDisplayName,""))))
      | eval cluster=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(properties_resourceName,properties_clusterName,_ResourceId,"unknown_cluster"))))
      | where match(rawl,"repairnode") OR match(rawl,"cloudprovisioningservices.repairnode") OR match(rawl,"aks_maintenanceunscheduled")
      | eval cloud_plane="azure"
      | eval repair_class="managed_cloud_repair"
      | eval repair_detail=case(match(rawl,"repairnode"),"aks_repair_node", true(),"aks_unscheduled_maintenance_signal")
      | eval node_pool=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(properties_agentPool,properties_nodepool,properties_nodePool,"unknown_pool"))))
      | eval instance_hint=trim(toString(coalesce(properties_vmId,properties_instanceId,"")))
      | eval node_name=""
      | fields _time cluster node_pool cloud_plane repair_class repair_detail instance_hint node_name ]
    [ search (index=k8s_logs OR index=k8s OR index=containers) (sourcetype="kube:container:logs" OR sourcetype="karpenter" OR sourcetype="otel:logs") earliest=-24h@h latest=now
      | eval rawl=lower(_raw)
      | eval cluster=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(k8s_cluster_name,cluster_name,cluster,"unknown_cluster"))))
      | where (like(rawl,"%karpenter%") OR sourcetype="karpenter") AND match(rawl,"disruption") AND (match(rawl,"nodeclaim") OR match(rawl,"node_claim") OR match(rawl,"drift") OR match(rawl,"underutilized") OR match(rawl,"empty") OR match(rawl,"expired") OR match(rawl,"health"))
      | eval cloud_plane="karpenter"
      | eval repair_class=case(match(rawl,"drift") OR match(rawl,"underutilized") OR match(rawl,"empty") OR match(rawl,"expired"),"karpenter_optimization_disruption", true(),"karpenter_health_or_ambiguous")
      | eval repair_detail=case(match(rawl,"drift"),"karpenter_drift", match(rawl,"underutilized"),"karpenter_underutilized", match(rawl,"empty"),"karpenter_empty", match(rawl,"expired"),"karpenter_expired", match(rawl,"health"),"karpenter_health", true(),"karpenter_disruption_other")
      | eval node_pool=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(karpenter_nodepool,node_pool,provisioner_name,"karpenter_aggregate"))))
      | rex field=_raw max_match=0 "(?i)node[:=\\s]+(?<kn>[a-z0-9\\.\\-]+)"
      | eval node_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(kn,k8s_node_name,""))))
      | eval instance_hint=""
      | fields _time cluster node_pool cloud_plane repair_class repair_detail instance_hint node_name ]
| fillnull value="unknown_cluster" cluster
| fillnull value="unknown_pool" node_pool
| fillnull value="" node_name instance_hint
| sort 0 cluster, node_pool, + _time
| streamstats time_window=3600s count AS repairs_rolling_1h BY cluster node_pool
| eval repair_storm_flag=if(repairs_rolling_1h>=5,1,0)
| eventstats count AS repairs_per_pool_day BY cluster node_pool
| join type=left max=0 cluster node_pool
    [| search index=k8s_metrics sourcetype="prometheus:scrape:metrics" earliest=-24h@h latest=now
      | eval cluster=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(k8s_cluster_name,cluster_name,cluster,eks_cluster_name,gke_cluster_name,aks_cluster_name,resource_cluster,""))))
      | eval mn=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(__name__,metric_name,name,""))))
      | eval nn=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(node,node_name,kubernetes_io_hostname,k8s_node_name,""))))
      | rex field=_raw "node_group=\"(?<nglab>[^\"]+)\""
      | eval node_pool=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(nglab,label_eks_nodegroup,label_node_group,node_group,nodegroup,""))))
      | where mn="kube_node_info" AND len(cluster)>0 AND len(nn)>0 AND len(node_pool)>0
      | stats dc(nn) AS kube_pool_live_nodes BY cluster node_pool ]
| join type=left max=0 cluster node_name
    [| search index=k8s_metrics sourcetype="prometheus:scrape:metrics" earliest=-24h@h latest=now
      | eval cluster=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(k8s_cluster_name,cluster_name,cluster,eks_cluster_name,gke_cluster_name,aks_cluster_name,resource_cluster,""))))
      | eval mn=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(__name__,metric_name,name,""))))
      | eval nn=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(node,node_name,kubernetes_io_hostname,k8s_node_name,""))))
      | where mn="kube_node_status_condition" AND len(cluster)>0 AND len(nn)>0
      | rex field=_raw "condition=\"(?<cond>[^\"]+)\""
      | rex field=_raw "status=\"(?<st>[^\"]+)\""
      | where cond="Ready" AND lower(st)="false"
      | stats latest(_time) AS last_ready_false BY cluster nn
      | rename nn AS node_name ]
| join type=left max=0 cluster instance_hint node_name
    [| inputlookup node_instance_launched.csv
      | eval cluster=lower(trim(toString(cluster)))
      | eval instance_hint=trim(toString(coalesce(instance_hint,instance_id,vm_id,"")))
      | eval node_name=lower(trim(toString(coalesce(node_name,node,""))))
      | eval launch_epoch=tonumber(tostring(coalesce(launch_epoch,created_epoch,"0")),10)
      | fields cluster instance_hint node_name launch_epoch ]
| eval premature_replace_flag=if(isnotnull(launch_epoch) AND launch_epoch>0 AND (_time-launch_epoch)<3600 AND _time>launch_epoch,1,0)
| eval followup_notready_risk_sec=if(isnotnull(last_ready_false), round(_time-last_ready_false,0), null())
| eval workload_rca_note=if(len(node_name)>0,printf("map kube_pod_info node=%s for workload owners",node_name),"attach kube_pod_info join for workload RCA")
| eval dm_key="uc3244"
| join type=left max=0 dm_key
    [| tstats count AS change_window_hits FROM datamodel=Change WHERE nodename=Change earliest=-24h@h latest=now
      | eval dm_key="uc3244" ]
| fields - dm_key
| fillnull value=0 change_window_hits kube_pool_live_nodes repair_storm_flag premature_replace_flag
| eval severity=case(repair_storm_flag==1 AND repair_class=="managed_cloud_repair","high_repair_storm_cloud", repair_storm_flag==1 AND cloud_plane=="karpenter","medium_karpenter_burst_review", premature_replace_flag==1 AND repair_class=="managed_cloud_repair","high_premature_cloud_replace", match(repair_detail,"karpenter_health"),"medium_karpenter_health_review", repairs_per_pool_day>=12 AND repair_class=="managed_cloud_repair","medium_elevated_daily_repair_rate", true(),"low_informational_repair_event")
| table _time cluster node_pool cloud_plane repair_class repair_detail repairs_rolling_1h repair_storm_flag repairs_per_pool_day kube_pool_live_nodes premature_replace_flag followup_notready_risk_sec workload_rca_note change_window_hits severity
```

savedsearches.conf sketch:

```
[uc_3_2_44_managed_node_auto_repair_storm]
cron_schedule = 10 * * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
enableSched = 1
action.email = 1
action.email.to = platform-k8s@example.com
action.email.subject = Node repair storm $result.cluster$ pool $result.node_pool$
counttype = number of events
relation = greater than
quantity = 0
search = | savedsearch uc_3_2_44_managed_node_auto_repair_storm | where repair_storm_flag>0 OR match(severity,"^high_")
```

Step 4 — Validate

Positive test A — AWS: in a lab account with a disposable managed node group, use supported APIs or console actions that trigger RecycleNode or unhealthy instance replacement while CloudTrail remains enabled; confirm aws:cloudtrail events arrive within minutes, cluster and nodegroup fields populate under your extractions, and uc_3_2_44_managed_node_auto_repair_storm returns repair_detail containing eks_mng_recycle_node or adjacent strings.

Positive test B — GKE: in a non-production cluster with node auto-repair enabled, consult Google documentation for safe simulation patterns and confirm google:gcp:audit carries node_auto_repair text with compute delete semantics; verify repair_detail reads gke_node_auto_repair_delete.

Positive test C — Azure: in a sandbox subscription, capture AKS repair or maintenance signals that your tenant logs as azure:auditlogs and confirm aks_repair_node or aks_unscheduled_maintenance_signal classifications appear.

Positive test D — Karpenter: in a lab cluster, induce a disruption decision that logs Drift or Health reasons; confirm karpenter or kube:container:logs arms populate repair_class distinctly for optimization versus health-like narratives.

Storm math: replay five synthetic timestamps inside one hour for the same cluster and node_pool in a csv-based load test index and confirm repairs_rolling_1h reaches five and repair_storm_flag equals one.

Negative test — Voluntary drain only: run kubectl cordon and drain without cloud recycle events; UC-3.2.33 should fire while this UC stays quiet on managed_cloud_repair rows, proving separation.

Negative test — Scale-out failure only: reproduce Pending backlog with CA errors; UC-3.2.46 should fire without managed repair audit rows here.

Field sanity: rename a sandbox forwarder field to a camelCase-only variant and confirm coalesce ladders still resolve cluster and pool names.

Performance: use Job Inspector during peak ingest; if multisearch exceeds SLA, materialize hourly summary indexes per cloud sourcetype keyed on cluster and node_pool.

Step 5 — Operationalize & Troubleshoot

Case 1 — repair_storm_flag with managed_cloud_repair and concentrated timestamps: open a cloud bridge, check AMI, kubelet, CNI, and ENI health dashboards for the pool AZ, and consider pausing rollouts until root cause is known.

Case 2 — high_premature_cloud_replace rows clustered after an image bump: roll back to the prior image or freeze node pool upgrades while vendor cases progress.

Case 3 — medium_karpenter_burst_review dominated by Drift and Underutilized: treat as optimization noise unless customer impact coincides; coordinate with FinOps before muting.

Case 4 — medium_karpenter_health_review with Health reasons: prioritize kubelet logs, instance store, and provider hardware events on the affected pool.

Case 5 — followup_notready_risk_sec under six hundred seconds repeatedly: investigate replacement bootstrap, join timeouts, and CNI attach on new nodes before blaming applications.

Case 6 — kube_pool_live_nodes drops sharply while repairs_rolling_1h rises: declare capacity risk, verify quotas, and confirm autoscaler siblings are not simultaneously failing scale-out.

Case 7 — Azure-only storms during regional degradation: broaden to network provider status pages and compare AZ skew in raw azure:auditlogs.

Case 8 — AWS-only storms after security agent rollout: correlate with daemonset releases and instance metadata throttling.

Case 9 — GCP-only storms after maintenance windows: validate auto-repair overlap with published infrastructure events before executive escalation.

Case 10 — change_window_hits elevated without matching CAB tickets: invoke change audit procedures and pause ambiguous automation.

Case 11 — inputlookup join misses causing premature_replace_flag stuck at zero: fix inventory job latency or widen correlation keys to instance ARNs; never treat absence as proof of healthy age.

Case 12 — dashboard shows karpenter_health spikes while cloud audit is silent: validate that Karpenter is the acting disruption layer and that cloud recycle is intentionally suppressed by design in that cluster model.

Operational cadence: weekly review of false-positive themes, monthly reconciliation with cloud console repair history, quarterly drill tying this UC to UC-3.2.3 NotReady replay scenarios.

Closing checklist: five em-dash step headers appear exactly as required; Step 3 includes fenced SPL identical to the spl JSON field; Step 5 lists twelve case lines; monitoringType lists Reliability and Availability; cimModels lists Change and Application_State; equipmentModels lists kubernetes_k8s; references include Google GKE node auto-repair, Amazon EKS managed node groups, Microsoft AKS auto-repair, Karpenter disruption, EKS logging and monitoring, AWS EKS control plane logging, and Splunk Connect for Syslog sources documentation.

Telemetry depth addendum for platform leads: when dual stacks ingest the same kube-state-metrics endpoint through redundant collectors, enforce deduplication or drop duplicate scrapes so kube_pool_live_nodes is not doubled. For OpenShift, map node pool labels carefully because machine-set names differ from cloud node group names. For air-gapped environments, expect delayed CloudTrail batch files; widen alert windows slightly and monitor ingest lag. For multi-tenant Splunk, keep macros per tenant that map index names without forking core SPL. For regulated industries, store evidence exports with hashed node names when policy forbids clear text. For Windows node pools, confirm repair audit parity before enabling Azure or AWS arms. For GitOps managed pools, tie node_pool strings to Terraform workspace outputs in the lookup to reduce naming drift.

Runbook micro-sections: if severity is high_repair_storm_cloud, attach the cloud support case id in the notable. If severity is high_premature_cloud_replace, link to the image build record and CIS benchmark delta. If Karpenter and managed cloud signals overlap during migrations, annotate the bridge with the declared cutover phase.

FinOps alignment: optimization-class Karpenter disruption should not silently fund unnecessary cloud cases; tag Drift bursts with savings program identifiers when appropriate.

Reliability alignment: pair with UC-3.2.3 when customers report application errors during repair storms to prove whether NotReady overlap exists.

Security alignment: verify CloudTrail and Activity readers cannot exfiltrate secrets embedded in rare error payloads; redact in props.conf when discovered.

Performance alignment: keep earliest at twenty-four hours for dashboards and consider seven-day clones for postmortems only.

Operations alignment: document who may freeze node pools after hours versus who must approve FinOps tradeoffs on Karpenter consolidation.

Capacity alignment: chart repairs_per_pool_day alongside pending backlog panels from UC-3.2.46 even though pending is not this UC’s primary axis.

Training drills: semi-annually rehearse interpreting repair_detail taxonomy with new engineers; quarterly rehearse storm bridges with cloud vendor liaisons.

Audit evidence: export weekly CSV of the closing table to a restricted index with lookup commit hashes and cloud query identifiers stored in the description field.

Governance: record exceptions when business accepts elevated repair rates during fleet-wide OS migrations; annotate node_pool rows in node_instance_launched.csv with exception_expiry when needed.

Closing narrative: this UC closes the gap between opaque node churn and actionable repair intelligence by demanding cloud audit, Karpenter context, kube_node_info grounding, and optional birth-time lookups in one analyst row so leaders spend minutes—not hours—deciding whether to freeze images, open cloud cases, or redirect FinOps attention.
grandmaExplanation: We watch when the cloud automatically swaps out unhealthy worker machines in your container fleet, how often it happens in each group, and whether those swaps bunch up in a scary burst. We also notice when a brand-new machine gets replaced almost immediately, which often means something deeper is wrong.
hardware: Applies to Amazon EKS managed node groups, Google GKE Standard node pools with node auto-repair, Microsoft AKS node auto-repair, and clusters using Karpenter for disruption-driven replacements on Linux x86_64 or Arm64 worker fleets when audit and metrics field parity holds.
